method_atom: A local-density (slowly-varying external potential) treatment that combines a self-consistent mean-field (Hartree-Fock) description of interacting units with the prediction for a topological, continuous-symmetry phase transition of an infinite uniform two-dimensional system, further corrected for the residual thermal excitation of a strongly frozen degree of freedom and benchmarked against an exact stochastic (Monte-Carlo) computation. The transition it captures separates a high-parameter single-component disordered phase from a low-parameter bimodal, quasi-ordered phase as a control parameter crosses a critical value, and it predicts a strong shift of the critical population relative to the non-interacting theory.
object_atom: A dilute interacting gas of bosonic rubidium atoms harmonically confined in a quasi-two-dimensional geometry, spanning the crossover from Bose-Einstein condensation to Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless physics, characterized by its spatial density profiles, critical atom number, and high-contrast matter-wave interference between independent clouds.
brief_reason: The transferable method is the local-density-approximation coupling of mean-field theory to a topological 2D phase transition validated against Monte-Carlo, while the harmonically trapped quasi-2D bosonic rubidium gas is the physics-specific object.
